title: Gait-based Authentication: Evaluation of Energy Consumption on Commercial Devices
venue: WristSense 2022: Eighth Workshop on Sensing Systems and Applications Using Wrist Worn Smart Devices

method.input_representation: Acceleration features extracted from x, y, z components and magnitude (Euclidean norm)
method.training_strategy: Semi-supervised anomaly detection based on distance to the legitimate user's pattern

evaluation.context: EER is below 5% when a sampling frequency of at least 25 Hz is used

evaluation_protocol: Comparing average EER across 20 volunteers across different sampling frequencies (12.5, 25, 50, 100 Hz)

reported_limitations: Results are influenced by the specific application and experimental setup
Performance degrades significantly at low sampling frequencies (e.g., 12.5 Hz)

usability.usability_issues: Energy consumption increases proportionally with sampling frequency; computation offloading via Bluetooth is less energy-efficient than local execution.

key_contribution: Evaluated the trade-off between sampling frequency and authentication accuracy/energy consumption on commercial wearable devices
Demonstrated that local computation is more energy-efficient than offloading to a smartphone due to BLE transmission costs
main_strength: Implementation on commercial hardware (Android Wear OS)
Quantified energy impact of different architectural configurations
main_weakness: Accuracy evaluation relied on pre-collected traces rather than real-time interaction
No spoofing or active attack testing
insights.notes_for_review: The paper highlights that for gait authentication, the energy cost of the radio (BLE) for offloading exceeds the energy cost of local CPU processing on modern smartwatches.
